title: Digital's top finance chief quits (DEC CFO William Steul resigns)
author: Aaron Zitner (The Boston Globe)
abstract: Boston Globe (May 1994) reports DEC CFO William M. Steul, 51, resigned a month after DEC's stock plummeted on a $183M unexpected Q3 loss. Second top-rank departure since the April 15 announcement (Edward E. Lucente, worldwide sales chief and #2 exec, left days after). Vincent J. Mullarkey, 46, corporate controller (Bentley College 1971, joined DEC same year), promoted to CFO. Quote from Franc Romano of Aberdeen Group (former DEC employee) criticizes Mullarkey: 'The only financial experience Vin has ever had was with Digital. I don't believe he brings the broad perspective that is necessary there or the cash management skills the company is going to need.' Counter-quote from Richard Buchanan of Forrester Research: DEC's accounting system 'is so arcane and quite specific to the history that Digital has passed through. To bring in an outsider to decipher that would be tough to do' but 'is broken in many ways and needs to be fixed.' Steul was 22-year DEC veteran, two years as CFO; predecessor was James Osterhoff (Ford Motor recruit, highly regarded for financial discipline). Mullarkey was Robert Palmer's controller in DEC semiconductor group.
importance_rationale: Aberdeen Group quote on DEC executive turmoil from Franc Romano (former DEC employee, current Aberdeen analyst) — captures Aberdeen's role as analyst on the most-watched local-Boston-area tech-industry event of 1994 (DEC's decline). Important for the Aberdeen analyst-bench memoir context and Kastner's relationship with DEC (he later worked at DEC briefly per cache).
relevance_rationale: DEC long acquired by Compaq (1998) then HP (2002). The CFO transition is historical. Modern relevance: cautionary tale of company-specific accounting systems and inability to forecast quarterly results.
prescience_rationale: Romano's skepticism about DEC's ability to recover with insider-only leadership was vindicated: DEC continued to lose money through 1994-1996, Mullarkey could not reverse the decline, and DEC was sold to Compaq in 1998 (then HP 2002). The article's concern about 'broken' financial systems presaged the broader disclosure-quality issues that would later (2002) drive Sarbanes-Oxley reform.
